name: Jersey Devil
description: A legendary creature said to haunt the Pine Barrens of New Jersey, often described as a winged, hoofed devil-like beast.

name: Dover Demon
description: A strange humanoid creature reportedly seen in Dover, Massachusetts, in 1977, known for its large head, hairless body, and glowing eyes.

name: Bigfoot
description: North America's best-known cryptid, usually described as a large, hairy ape-like humanoid sighted throughout the Pacific Northwest.

name: Skinwalker
description: A feared figure from Navajo tradition, often described as a dangerous witch or shapeshifter capable of taking animal form.

name: Mothman
description: A mysterious winged being with glowing red eyes that was reported many times around Point Pleasant, West Virginia, in the 1960s.

name: Bunny Man
description: A local Virginia legend about a rabbit-suited figure carrying an axe and appearing near a bridge in Fairfax County.

name: Lizard Man of Scape Ore Swamp
description: A reptilian humanoid from South Carolina folklore, usually described as green-scaled, red-eyed, and connected to swamp encounters.

name: Loveland Frogman
description: An Ohio cryptid said to be a human-sized amphibian creature seen near the river in Loveland.

name: Champ (Lake Champlain Monster)
description: A famous lake monster said to inhabit Lake Champlain, often compared to the Loch Ness Monster and reported since the 19th century.

name: Rougarou
description: A werewolf-like creature from Louisiana bayou folklore rooted in Cajun and French traditions.

name: Chupacabra
description: The 'goat-sucker,' a livestock-attacking cryptid associated with Puerto Rico and later widely reported in Texas and the American South.

name: Fresno Nightcrawler
description: A bizarre figure captured in footage near Fresno, California, appearing like a pair of walking white legs with an unusual gait.

name: Beast of Bray Road
description: A werewolf-like monster from Wisconsin folklore, often described as a half-human, half-wolf creature seen on rural roads.

name: Flatwoods Monster
description: A strange being linked to a 1952 West Virginia UFO event, described as tall, glowing-eyed, and helmet-headed.

name: Thunderbird
description: A gigantic bird from Indigenous traditions, with later reports in the Midwest and Alaska describing an enormous flying creature.

name: Goatman
description: A half-human, half-goat monster from Maryland folklore, often imagined as an axe-wielding figure roaming at night.

name: Ozark Howler
description: A black beast from the Ozark Mountains known for glowing red eyes and a terrifying howl.

name: Hopkinsville Goblins
description: The creatures from the famous 1955 Kentucky encounter, often described as small alien- or goblin-like beings attacking a farmhouse.

name: Van Meter Visitor
description: A flying monster from a 1903 Iowa flap, remembered as a large winged creature with glowing features.

name: Enfield Horror
description: A bizarre red-eyed creature from Illinois folklore, described in 1973 as a strange three-legged being with an eerie cry.

name: Altamaha-ha
description: A river monster from Georgia legend, commonly described as a long-necked aquatic creature living in the Altamaha River.

name: Honey Island Swamp Monster
description: A Louisiana swamp cryptid often portrayed as a hulking half-human, half-beast creature.

name: White River Monster
description: An Arkansas lake and river monster reported since the early 20th century in the White River area.

name: Skunk Ape
description: Florida's regional Bigfoot variant, named for the strong odor witnesses say surrounds it.

name: Pamola
description: A bird-like spirit from Maine tradition, believed to guard Mount Katahdin.

name: Wood Devils
description: Tall, gray, human-shaped beings said to roam the forests of New Hampshire in regional folklore.

name: Melon Heads
description: A New England-style urban legend about small humanoids with oversized heads that appear in rural roads and woods.

name: Palatine Light
description: A mysterious ghost light associated with local legends in New York, often interpreted as a supernatural or cryptid-adjacent phenomenon.

name: Beast of Bladenboro
description: A catlike monster from North Carolina lore, blamed for livestock killings and nighttime attacks in the 1950s.

name: White Thang
description: A pale, fast-moving shaggy beast from northern Alabama folklore, often described as eerie and almost ghostlike.

name: Snallygaster
description: A dragon-like creature from Maryland folklore, usually described with wings, claws, and a terrifying screech.

name: Chessie
description: A sea-serpent-like cryptid associated with Chesapeake Bay sightings.

name: Fouke Monster
description: A large hairy swamp creature from Arkansas, closely related to southern Bigfoot-style legends.

name: Oklahoma Octopus
description: A lake monster from Oklahoma folklore said to dwell underwater and attack swimmers.

name: Mogollon Monster
description: Arizona's Bigfoot-like cryptid, described as a tall, foul-smelling, hairy humanoid of the Mogollon Rim.

name: Slide-Rock Bolter
description: A famous Rocky Mountain fearsome critter said to slide down slopes and swallow whole groups of people or animals.

name: Bear Lake Monster
description: A lake monster from the Utah-Idaho border, reported in Bear Lake since the 19th century.

name: Tahoe Tessie
description: The legendary aquatic creature of Lake Tahoe, often imagined as a long-necked lake monster.

name: Sharlie
description: Idaho's best-known lake monster, said to live in Payette Lake and resemble a serpentine beast.

name: Shunka Warakin
description: A strange predator from frontier-era accounts in the northern Plains, usually described as wolf-like but unlike any known animal.

name: Flathead Lake Monster
description: Montana's famous lake cryptid, often compared to other North American lake monsters.

name: Michigan Dogman
description: A dog- or wolf-like cryptid from Michigan folklore, said to walk upright and possess humanlike menace.

name: Momo
description: The Missouri Monster, a hairy humanoid cryptid reported near the Mississippi River region in the 1970s.

name: Wendigo
description: A terrifying cannibal spirit or monster from Algonquian tradition, later often represented in popular cryptid culture as a gaunt humanoid creature.

name: Batsquatch
description: A winged cryptid from Washington State, often described as a bat-like creature with primate features near Mount St. Helens.

name: Colossal Claude
description: A giant sea serpent said to inhabit the waters near Oregon's coast.

name: Tizheruk
description: An enormous sea-serpent-like creature from Alaska Native tradition, said to live in cold northern waters.

name: Kushtaka
description: A dangerous shapeshifting being from Tlingit tradition in southeast Alaska, often described as part otter and part human.

name: Menehune
description: The legendary small people of Hawaiian folklore, said to be master builders who work in secret.

name: Ogopogo
description: A famous long-bodied lake monster of the Pacific Northwest cultural sphere, often described as serpentine and elusive.

name: Jackalope
description: A playful Western folklore creature imagined as a jackrabbit with antelope horns.

name: Kansas River Monster
description: A large water cryptid said to inhabit stretches of the Kansas River in local legend.

name: Nebraska Alkali Lake Monster
description: A regional lake monster from Nebraska folklore, associated with isolated waters and strange sightings.

name: Shennongjia Wildman (神农架野人)
description: A Chinese wildman tradition from Shennongjia, describing a large, ape-like humanoid said to roam remote forests.

name: Kanas Lake Monster (喀纳斯湖‘大红鱼’)
location: Kanas Lake, Altay Prefecture, Xinjiang, China
description: A monster tradition from Kanas Lake in Xinjiang, often tied to giant reddish fish-like beings seen in deep water.

name: Tianchi Monster (长白山天池水怪)
location: Heaven Lake, Changbai Mountain, Jilin, China
description: A long-running lake monster legend from Heaven Lake on Changbai Mountain, with reports describing a large unknown aquatic animal.

name: Qinghai Lake Monster (青海湖水怪)
description: A lake monster tradition from Qinghai Lake, usually described as a large unidentified creature seen breaking the water's surface.

name: Jingpo Lake Monster (镜泊湖水怪)
description: A northeastern Chinese lake monster legend associated with unusual large aquatic sightings in Jingpo Lake.

name: ‘Laoye Temple’ Waters Phenomena (鄱阳湖老爷庙水域异常浪涌/水怪传说)
location: Laoye Temple Waters, Poyang Lake, Jiangxi, China
description: A cluster of strange-water and monster legends from the Laoye Temple waters of Poyang Lake, long linked to disappearances, sudden waves, and unexplained danger.

name: Shuihouzi (水猴子)
location: Lower Yangtze and Dongting-Tai Lake region, China
description: A Chinese water-spirit cryptid often blamed for drownings, usually imagined as a lurking aquatic humanoid that drags people underwater.

name: Three-Legged Beast (三足兽)
description: A strange three-legged beast from classical Chinese anecdotal lore, remembered as an uncanny and ominous animal.

name: Flying Fox (飞狐，非蝙蝠)
location: North of the Great Wall, Hebei-Shanxi border region, China
description: A fox-like flying creature from classical strange-tales literature, distinct from the real-world bat sometimes called a flying fox.

name: Fruit-Leopard (果下豹)
description: A miniature leopard-like beast from old Chinese records, notable for its unusually small body and exotic reputation.

name: Sea Monk (海和尚)
location: Bohai and Yellow Sea coast, China
description: A monk-like sea cryptid from Chinese anecdotal lore, blending marine imagery with supernatural storytelling.

name: One-legged Serpent (一足蛇)
description: A one-legged snake-like creature from traditional Chinese strange-tales, remembered as an unnatural and ominous beast.

name: Earth-Burrow Beast (土窟异兽)
location: Loess cave region of North China
description: A subterranean cryptid from Qing-era writings, described as a strange beast lurking in caves or earthen burrows.

name: Dongting Lake ‘Water Ape’ Cluster (洞庭湖水怪/水猴子传说群)
description: A cluster of water-monster and water-ape stories from Dongting Lake, where folklore often merges with the dangers of river and lake travel.

name: Qiangtang Plateau ‘Yeti’ Reports (羌塘/藏区雪人/野人报告)
location: Ngari and Qiangtang Plateau, Tibet, China
description: Modern reports from the Tibetan Plateau about unknown large humanoids or 'wild men,' often compared to the Yeti.

name: Yingkou Fallen Dragon (营口坠龙)
description: The famous 1934 Yingkou incident in which witnesses claimed that a dragon-like creature fell to earth, though later explanations suggested a beached marine animal.

name: Donkey-Headed Wolf (驴头狼)
description: A folkloric beast from Chinese regional legend, described as having a donkey's head and a wolf's body and appearing at night.

name: Red Willow Child (红柳娃)
description: A small mysterious being from Xinjiang folklore, said to dwell among red willow thickets and associated with eerie sounds and desert mystery.

name: Pukwudgie
description: A small but dangerous trickster being from Northeastern Indigenous and regional folklore, often tied to the Bridgewater Triangle.

name: Glawackus
description: A Connecticut cryptid described as an eerie mix of cat, dog, and bear seen at night in rural areas.

name: Lake Worth Monster
description: A Texas monster legend centered on a half-human, half-goat creature reported around Lake Worth.

name: Pressie (Presque Isle Monster)
description: A Pennsylvania lake monster associated with Presque Isle Bay and often compared to other famous aquatic cryptids.

name: Wampus Cat
description: A monstrous wild cat from Appalachian folklore, known for glowing eyes and terrifying nighttime appearances.

name: Big Muddy Monster
description: A Southern Illinois hairy humanoid cryptid whose sightings made it one of the Midwest's classic Bigfoot-like legends.

name: Thetor
description: A Kansas folklore creature described as a huge reptilian or dragon-like beast seen near rivers and rural roads.

name: Elkhorn Lake Monster
description: A Nebraska regional lake monster, usually described as a large shadowy or serpentine creature in the water.

name: Monster of Pocomoonshine Lake
description: A serpent-like lake monster from rural Maine folklore associated with isolated northern waters.

name: Block Island Sea Serpent
description: A Rhode Island sea-serpent legend with 19th-century reports of a gigantic snake-like marine creature.

name: Gloucester Sea Serpent
description: One of New England's most famous sea-serpent traditions, centered on repeated reports off Gloucester and Cape Ann.

name: Moon-Eyed People
description: A mysterious pale, large-eyed people from Appalachian legend in Georgia, said to avoid sunlight.

name: Letiche
description: A Louisiana folkloric monster from French-Creole tradition, often imagined as a bizarre winged or shapeshifting night creature.

name: Mississippi River Monster
description: A large aquatic cryptid said to inhabit stretches of the Mississippi River and appear as a dark, serpentine beast.
